words: Sister Mary Christina, 1976-08-19, Interview - MP3 Recording
Lee Magnuson, Interviewer
Latah County Oral History Collection, University of Idaho Library
Transcription by: Courtney Berge
[Small talk while someone speaks on the telephone]
[Interview starts at 2:05]

speaker: SMC
words: Well, Mr. and Mrs. McConnell came from the east and they met over here

speaker: SMC
words: in Oregon. That's Mrs. Borah's parents. They were married in Salem,

speaker: SMC
words: Oregon, then they moved to California and Mrs. Borah was born in

speaker: SMC
words: Arcata?, but in articles many of them say she was born in Eureka, but

speaker: SMC
words: Arcata? is just a suburb of Eureka. Then they came back, she lived in

speaker: SMC
words: Salem as a child. Then moved to Yamhill in Oregon and Mr. McConnell came

speaker: SMC
words: here to Idaho and built this mansion. And they moved in the day before

speaker: SMC
words: Christmas in 1886, and they had an open house after they moved in. And

speaker: SMC
words: although Mrs. Borah was at such an advanced age, she remembered the name

speaker: SMC
words: of a lady by the name of Mrs. Johnson who went through the house and she

speaker: SMC
words: saw the lavatory, and said, "The idea, a privy in the house, I'll never

speaker: SMC
words: come in here again!"

speaker: SMC
words: And then later on, before they went to Idaho and lived here in Oregon,

speaker: SMC
words: little Mary became ill. The doctor didn't know what was wrong and

speaker: SMC
words: Grandma said, Grandma lived with them, "I'd like to take her to

speaker: SMC
words: Portland. I know a doctor there that could help her." So Grandma took

speaker: SMC
words: Mary McConnell on the train to Portland. And on the way there was a lady

speaker: SMC
words: across the aisle staring at the baby. When they got to Portland, Grandma

speaker: SMC
words: went to Portland Hotel, its torn down now it was a beautiful old fashion

speaker: SMC
words: hotel where all the dignitaries who came to town went. So Grandma went

speaker: SMC
words: to find a room, and she put the baby on the bed, and they didn't have

speaker: SMC
words: bathrooms by each room in those days, so she put the baby in the bed and

speaker: SMC
words: left to go to the bathroom. Came back, the baby was gone. So she rushed

speaker: SMC
words: down stairs and said, "Did you see anybody carrying a baby?" She

speaker: SMC
words: described the type of blanket or whatever was around the child. "Yes, we

speaker: SMC
words: saw a lady carry that baby out." So they called the police right away

speaker: SMC
words: and went out searching and they found a lady holding the baby about a

speaker: SMC
words: few blocks away and they could tell by the look of the lady that she

speaker: SMC
words: wasn't normal. They found out later that she had lost a child and she

speaker: SMC
words: saw this child and she thought that was hers. So, if they hadn't found

speaker: SMC
words: her she would have had an entirely different life than what she had.

speaker: SMC
words: So, then in one picture I have here, maybe some of you have seen the

speaker: SMC
words: picture, she has a necklace of hand carved elephants. They're all ivory.

speaker: SMC
words: And she said an old Indian here in Idaho carved them and gave them to

speaker: SMC
words: her when she was a child.

speaker: SMC
words: And then she, I think you know that part of it, that she met Mr. Borah

speaker: SMC
words: when her father became governor of Idaho and they were married. Then

speaker: SMC
words: they went to Washington D.C. in 1907. And they were there, not very

speaker: SMC
words: long, when Mr. Borah became very prominent in the senate. He had a very,

speaker: SMC
words: very wonderful way of speaking. He was already becoming prominent in

speaker: SMC
words: Washington D.C.. He came home one evening and Mary said, "We got an

speaker: SMC
words: invitation to go to the White House, we're not going are we?" "We

speaker: SMC
words: certainly are," he said, "to get an invitation from the White House is

speaker: SMC
words: practically a command." "But I have nothing to wear." You know how most

speaker: SMC
words: women will say, right? I have nothing to wear. "Well," he said, "get

speaker: SMC
words: something to wear." So she went over to town to get a dress, and found

speaker: SMC
words: one. She loved pink, so she had a hard time getting a dress to fit

speaker: SMC
words: because she was very small. I don't think she weighed more than 100

speaker: SMC
words: pounds. Along in the conversation they found out she was Senator Borah's

speaker: SMC
words: wife, and then one of the clerks ran to the propriety and said, "Just

speaker: SMC
words: think Senator Borah's wife is here buying an outfit to go to the White

speaker: SMC
words: House." So the proprietor came along and saw the pink dress and brings a

speaker: SMC
words: pair of pink shoes. And, "Oh, I can't afford that." "It's on the house."

speaker: SMC
words: So he gave her the shoes and gave her some accessories to go with this

speaker: SMC
words: pink dress.

speaker: SMC
words: So it was her first trip to the White House and Theodore Roosevelt was

speaker: SMC
words: President. And she was placed right beside him. Theodore Roosevelt was a

speaker: SMC
words: very hard person to entertain. It seems there was a man by the name of

speaker: SMC
words: Mr. White that Roosevelt mentioned. He knew something about a Mr. White.

speaker: SMC
words: He had read some book that he wrote. And then Mrs. Borah said, "You know

speaker: SMC
words: that Mr. White was a very dear friend of Billy's and he spent a vacation

speaker: SMC
words: at our house and he was writing that book and I named it." He had

speaker: SMC
words: proposed some names, I wish I could remember the name of the book.

speaker: SMC
words: Anyway, they talked about this book and after the meal was over they all

speaker: SMC
words: wondered what on earth she talked about to get that conversation with

speaker: SMC
words: the President. Because everyone said he was a very hard person to

speaker: SMC
words: converse with.

speaker: SMC
words: Now she knew all the Presidents' wives from Theodore Roosevelt on down

speaker: SMC
words: to Johnson. But she also knew the Nixons because the children, when they

speaker: SMC
words: were little girls, they lived in an apartment not far from the Borahs.

speaker: SMC
words: And when they first, maybe some of you remember the old electric cars,

speaker: SMC
words: they were electric cars that were invented were used around about 1912

speaker: SMC
words: along in those years. Some man gave her an electric car, it had her name

speaker: SMC
words: on the side of it. And she wanted to surprise, she always called her

speaker: SMC
words: husband Billy, so she had it behind the house, or behind the apartment,

speaker: SMC
words: and Mr. came home and she said, "I have a surprise for you." So she took

speaker: SMC
words: him outside to see the car and he didn't like it at first. She said,

speaker: SMC
words: "Well, he gave it to me and I couldn't say no and he already had my name

speaker: SMC
words: on it." So she often took Billy to the Congress, when he went as a

speaker: SMC
words: Senator to his work.

speaker: SMC
words: And she, of course, knew all the different ambassadors. And one day she

speaker: SMC
words: was out some place and there was a little child, son of a little

speaker: SMC
words: ambassador, and he was trying to think of her name. He said, "Oh, Little

speaker: SMC
words: Borah." So that's the way the term arrived, you've heard that "Little

speaker: SMC
words: Borah." That little boy called her "Little Borah" and that stuck. So she

speaker: SMC
words: was called "Little Borah" the rest of her life.

speaker: SMC
words: Now have you heard how Mr. Borah died? I haven't seen any article any

speaker: SMC
words: place, but she told me that Billy used to go out every morning. He loved

speaker: SMC
words: horses, so he took a horse with him to Washington D.C. and every morning

speaker: SMC
words: he'd go riding on his horse. So they always had breakfast at 6 o'clock

speaker: SMC
words: in the morning. This particular morning, she was already in her place.

speaker: SMC
words: She had a servant by the name of Daisy, a Negro lady she loved very

speaker: SMC
words: much. So she said, "Daisy, go see what's the matter with Billy." So she

speaker: SMC
words: went and he was in the bathroom and she called him, he didn't answer.

speaker: SMC
words: She said, "He's in the bathroom, he doesn't answer." So she says "Run

speaker: SMC
words: down the hall and get the doctor." They lived in a big apartment, so the

speaker: SMC
words: doctor came and he couldn't get the door open, so he got some other men

speaker: SMC
words: to help him open the door. And Billy had stepped out of the bathtub,

speaker: SMC
words: slipped, and knocked his head against the doorknob and he's lying there

speaker: SMC
words: unconscious. They picked him up and took him to the hospital and he died

speaker: SMC
words: three days later, he never came to. He was very active yet, 75 years

speaker: SMC
words: old, he was getting kind of old. At that time, that was in 1940, she was

speaker: SMC
words: 70 and he was 75.

speaker: SMC
words: I imagine you are all acquainted with that terrible disease she had here

speaker: SMC
words: in Idaho, the parrot fever. But there's one, the story she tells about

speaker: SMC
words: is different than the article, but there's one part that she tells I

speaker: SMC
words: think was very very beautiful. She said the nurse told her, that one day

speaker: SMC
words: while she was lying there unconscious, when she got the parrot fever it

speaker: SMC
words: was in July, and it was very hot, when she came to there was snow on the

speaker: SMC
words: ground, so she was sick that long. So when the nurse told her, after she

speaker: SMC
words: was better, one day the nurse went in the room and Billy was kneeling by

speaker: SMC
words: her bed and he was crying and he said, "Oh God, please, please let Mary

speaker: SMC
words: live. Don't let her die, I need her." And all the doctors said that she

speaker: SMC
words: would not live. Hardly anybody ever lived after they had parrot fever,

speaker: SMC
words: but finally one day she came to and the nurse said, "Are you speaking?"

speaker: SMC
words: and she said, "I'm hungry." And she ran out and called the doctors and

speaker: SMC
words: nurses, and she came back and Mrs. Borah said again, "I'm hungry." "Then

speaker: SMC
words: what would you like to eat?" "Mashed potatoes."

speaker: SMC
words: Then after Mr. Borah died, she came back to Idaho, but she stayed a very

speaker: SMC
words: short time because all her friends were gone. She didn't know any of the

speaker: SMC
words: people anymore around here, so she went back to Washington to live. She

speaker: SMC
words: lived there 'til about 1960, and she became very well interested. She

speaker: SMC
words: was lovely person, always wanted to do things for other people, never

speaker: SMC
words: thought of herself. So she went to the hospitals and visited soldiers

speaker: SMC
words: from Idaho and used to write to their mothers about their sons. She did

speaker: SMC
words: a beautiful job with these soldiers.

speaker: SMC
words: Then one day she was invited to a birthday party. Some lady lived in a

speaker: SMC
words: hotel, and this hotel had these revolving doors, they go around and you

speaker: SMC
words: get in a section and somebody gets in the next section, you've seen

speaker: SMC
words: those doors. Well she was in this section of the door and a young girl,

speaker: SMC
words: about 18, very heavy set, big girl, came in and gave that door a shove

speaker: SMC
words: and knocked Mrs. Borah against the wall. Now she was a little frail

speaker: SMC
words: person, and knocked her down, but she got up and they said, "Are you all

speaker: SMC
words: right?" And of course she was the kind that didn't want to give in, and

speaker: SMC
words: she said "Yes" and they helped her to the elevator. She went up to the

speaker: SMC
words: birthday party and they played games, and she managed to sit there at

speaker: SMC
words: the chair. Then they get up and they're going to the next room to look

speaker: SMC
words: at the birthday cake and she couldn't move. "Mrs. Borah are you ill?"

speaker: SMC
words: And then she told them what happened, they were gonna get an ambulance,

speaker: SMC
words: she says, "No, you call" she had a Negro chauffer, and she said, "You

speaker: SMC
words: call...Whatever his name was." So he came and picked her up and carried

speaker: SMC
words: her off to the car. He said, "Shall I take you to the hospital." "No,

speaker: SMC
words: take me home." So he took her home. And they called the doctor and the

speaker: SMC
words: doctor came and he was horrified when he saw her, but he said, "Now I'm

speaker: SMC
words: gonna get a nurse to sit with you because as soon as you think of

speaker: SMC
words: something you want, you'll get up and get it." So the nurse sat with her

speaker: SMC
words: until she went to sleep and the next morning when she awoke, she felt

speaker: SMC
words: something big on her side and she pulled up her gown. There was a big

speaker: SMC
words: blood clot under the skin and her leg was black all the way down. So she

speaker: SMC
words: called Daisy, "Look Daisy, what's this." And Daisy took one look and she

speaker: SMC
words: ran to the phone and called the doctor and the doctor came and they

speaker: SMC
words: brought an ambulance and they took her to the hospital. And she heard

speaker: SMC
words: the doctor say, "She survived parrot fever, but she'll never get over

speaker: SMC
words: this." But she did, she must have had a good heart. Anyway, she got over

speaker: SMC
words: it, and then that was shortly before, or around 1960.

speaker: SMC
words: So she was there, and she had two nieces in Portland, her sister Ollie

speaker: SMC
words: lived in Portland. And that's a question often asked, "How did Mrs.

speaker: SMC
words: Borah get to Portland and to Beaverton, why wasn't she in Idaho?" Well

speaker: SMC
words: Mrs. Lueddemann, was her sister's name, lived in Portland, and by the

speaker: SMC
words: way Mr. Lueddemann was a personal friend of Helen Keller. And Helen

speaker: SMC
words: Keller used to come to Portland to visit the Lueddemanns. And when Mr.

speaker: SMC
words: Lueddemann was ill, when these two girls, Mrs. Lueddemann had two

speaker: SMC
words: daughters, Mary and Joan, they lived part-time in Washington D.C. with

speaker: SMC
words: Mrs. Borah and went to school there. And when Mary, one of Mrs.

speaker: SMC
words: Lueddemann's daughters, came to visit Mrs. Borah she said, "Aunt Mamie,

speaker: SMC
words: your sick. I'm gonna take you to Portland with me." So Aunt Mamie was

speaker: SMC
words: happy to have somebody take care of her, I guess. So they sold, it's

speaker: SMC
words: sad, she had a collection of between two and three thousand elephants,

speaker: SMC
words: and she loved that collection very much, but she managed to bring a few

speaker: SMC
words: of them with her to Portland. But her things were sold, and she was

speaker: SMC
words: brought to Portland and lived with her sister for a while. And then her

speaker: SMC
words: sister became blind, so the two nieces put their mother and their aunt

speaker: SMC
words: in a nursing home. They were in a nursing home just a few years before

speaker: SMC
words: they came, they heard about Maryville, so they brought them to Maryville

speaker: SMC
words: in August 1966. And so she was there until she died January 1976. Ollie

speaker: SMC
words: died, I don't know just what year, but Ollie was 96 when she died.

speaker: SMC
words: Now in 1968 when Nixon was campaigning, he came to Portland. We have

speaker: SMC
words: primaries in May in Portland. He came to Portland to campaign, so I knew

speaker: SMC
words: what hotel they were staying in. So I called and asked for the person

speaker: SMC
words: who was taking care of, sort of secretary for Nixon. And so someone

speaker: SMC
words: answered, and they got Mrs. Nixon, so I talked to Mrs. Nixon for about

speaker: SMC
words: 15 minutes on the telephone, explained that I was a personal friend of

speaker: SMC
words: Mrs. Borah's. I said, "Now I know Mr. Nixon cannot campaign on election

speaker: SMC
words: day. Wouldn't it be nice if you would come out to Maryville and visit

speaker: SMC
words: Mrs. Borah because she knows you." And Mrs. Nixon said, "I'm very sorry,

speaker: SMC
words: but we're planned to go to Seattle tomorrow, election day, so we can't

speaker: SMC
words: come out." But she said, "Couldn't I talk to her on the phone." I said,

speaker: SMC
words: "No, that's not possible because she does not have a phone in her room

speaker: SMC
words: because her hearing is very bad. We'd have to take her quite a distance

speaker: SMC
words: to a phone and then she wouldn't understand you and it upsets her very

speaker: SMC
words: much to try to talk on the phone." I said, "but you could write to her."

speaker: SMC
words: So she wrote a letter. I had a chance to read the letter and she

speaker: SMC
words: mentioned me in that letter. And I was gonna ask her if I could have it,

speaker: SMC
words: but somebody stoled it, I never got it. I was sorry. I would have liked

speaker: SMC
words: to have had that letter in my book.

speaker: SMC
words: Then in November, her birthday's in October. That same year when Nixon

speaker: SMC
words: was campaigning for president, he knew when her birthday was, so he had

speaker: SMC
words: the telephone company, it must have been an expensive deal they did. The

speaker: SMC
words: telephone company came and established a special telephone in the living

speaker: SMC
words: room of Maryville, and Nixon knew the exact time they were gonna have

speaker: SMC
words: this party, it was broadcast on the TV and they had a loudspeaker on so

speaker: SMC
words: she could hear it. And he talked on the telephone direct to her and

speaker: SMC
words: wished her happy birthday while they could see him on the TV. Now they

speaker: SMC
words: planned that whole thing for her birthday, that was a big ad for him, I

speaker: SMC
words: guess, when he was campaigning.

speaker: SMC
words: Then when her 100th birthday came along. They made all kinds of

speaker: SMC
words: wonderful plans. First of all, there was a girl, a very beautiful girl,

speaker: SMC
words: Maureen Bassett, who graduated from our school. Her whole family just

speaker: SMC
words: loved Mrs. Borah, they went often to see her. Maureen was working for a

speaker: SMC
words: radio company, radio place in Hillsboro, so I told her, "Wouldn't it be

speaker: SMC
words: nice to have Mrs. Borah make a tape and have it on the radio." "Oh, that

speaker: SMC
words: would be wonderful!" So she had me go with her and we went over to Mrs.

speaker: SMC
words: Borah's room and she asked her questions. And she told the story of

speaker: SMC
words: getting that dress for her visit to the White House. She talked for 45

speaker: SMC
words: minutes on the radio, it turned out beautifully. Do you have that tape?

speaker: SMC
words: UNKNOWN 1: No

speaker: SMC
words: I must ask this niece, has the tape. And I'm gonna ask her if she

speaker: SMC
words: can't have another one made and give it to the museum because it was

speaker: SMC
words: just beautiful the way she spoke that whole 45 minutes. She was very

speaker: SMC
words: rational until she was about 102 and that was lovely. Well, then on her

speaker: SMC
words: actual birthday the Republican ladies from Portland planned a grand

speaker: SMC
words: affair. They had a bouquet, it was about that big around, one hundred

speaker: SMC
words: red roses. Oh, it was a gorgeous bouquet. And they had a big reception

speaker: SMC
words: for her, many people came. Nearly every one of her birthdays TV stations

speaker: SMC
words: would come and take pictures. Now for her 100^th^ birthday it was

speaker: SMC
words: broadcast around the world because there was another family that was

speaker: SMC
words: very much, many families just loved her, and these people were related

speaker: SMC
words: to one of our sisters and a boy, a young man, had been there to visit

speaker: SMC
words: and now he was in Vietnam and this came over the TV in Vietnam, and that

speaker: SMC
words: boy got so excited, "Oh, I know that lady, I know that lady, I saw her!"

speaker: SMC
words: He was so excited seeing that clear over in Vietnam from here. That must

speaker: SMC
words: have been exciting for the boy.

speaker: SMC
words: Now she was, well I'm just about finished now, but I can tell you more

speaker: SMC
words: if you want more, that's as much as I have down here. That she was loved

speaker: SMC
words: very very much. She was one of these persons that just everybody loved.

speaker: SMC
words: People'd come in and she liked, I'd put my arms around her and kiss her,

speaker: SMC
words: and oh she just loved it. She was just a darling. Had any of you see her

speaker: SMC
words: at all?

speaker: SMC
words: UNKNOWN 2: Yes, I did. I saw her many times

speaker: SMC
words: Did you come to Maryville?

speaker: SMC
words: UNKNOWN 2: No, but I saw her always when she came to Moscow. She used to

speaker: SMC
words: stay with me, she and Ollie Lueddemann. We were old friends. But I

speaker: SMC
words: wanted to tell you was, in the letters that she wrote she'd tell about

speaker: SMC
words: "my angel sisters" the angel sisters were the sisters at Maryville. My

speaker: SMC
words: angel sisters.

speaker: SMC
words: Well she was a person that was very outstanding. There are very few

speaker: SMC
words: in the world like her, that's true. Here's one experience she had, I

speaker: SMC
words: wasn't going to tell you this, but as long as I have more time, it was

speaker: SMC
words: strange, she had such a wonderful way of dealing with people. Some of

speaker: SMC
words: these boys that were insane, soldiers, she would go to visit them and

speaker: SMC
words: there were some, you know with different degrees of insanity, and she'd

speaker: SMC
words: kiss them and they call her mother. Some of the boys said, "If any of

speaker: SMC
words: the boys ever touch you, we'd kill 'em." And one boy that she liked very

speaker: SMC
words: very much was pleading with her to go to the airport. And she thought he

speaker: SMC
words: was more rational than he was, and so she begged the authorities, and

speaker: SMC
words: they said, "No, you'd better not." And she said, "But he wants to go so

speaker: SMC
words: badly." And Mrs. Borah had her own private chauffer, and they said,

speaker: SMC
words: "Well, all right then." So they sent another man along, one of the

speaker: SMC
words: orderlies from the insane asylum. And she was on one side of this boy

speaker: SMC
words: and this orderly on the other side, and he wanted to go to the airport,

speaker: SMC
words: and he behaved very beautifully on the way. Then when they got to the

speaker: SMC
words: airport he got out, then they could hardly get him back in the car. See

speaker: SMC
words: he planned, he was gonna get on a plane and go someplace. So they got

speaker: SMC
words: him back in the car and he fought all the way back. She said she was

speaker: SMC
words: just black and blue when she got back. And they told her, they warned

speaker: SMC
words: her you know, she never would try a thing like that again.

speaker: SMC
words: And she gave another example of how careful they should be. There was

speaker: SMC
words: some boy there in the asylum that seemed to be getting very normal. And

speaker: SMC
words: they thought he was all right, and the doctors thought he was all right

speaker: SMC
words: now to be sent out from the asylum. And between the asylum and another

speaker: SMC
words: building there was a tunnel underneath the street, and so he went down

speaker: SMC
words: through that tunnel, and when he got on the other side somebody saw him,

speaker: SMC
words: looked kind of wild eyes "Well, I took care of that, I took care of

speaker: SMC
words: her." And they wondered what he meant, so they went down. A nurse was

speaker: SMC
words: coming through the tunnel, he was going the other way, and he killed

speaker: SMC
words: her, and then he got out on the other side. They could see he was wild

speaker: SMC
words: eyed, you know, and they knew there was something wrong. And so they got

speaker: SMC
words: him back, I guess he never got out again, but that was the type of thing

speaker: SMC
words: that happened there. And so when Mrs. Borah was getting older and she

speaker: SMC
words: knew she couldn't do it anymore, she talked to a friend of hers and she

speaker: SMC
words: said, "You know the boys just love it when somebody comes and sees them.

speaker: SMC
words: Won't you come and see if you can take my place?" So the lady didn't

speaker: SMC
words: want to go, but Mrs. Borah asked her to, so she did. And the first day

speaker: SMC
words: she was there she was introducing this lady to some of the boys and she

speaker: SMC
words: turned her back and another boy knocked that woman down right away and

speaker: SMC
words: the woman screamed. And Mrs. Borah turned around and got the lady out of

speaker: SMC
words: there, but she never tried taking any other women in there. But not one

speaker: SMC
words: of those boys would touch Mrs. Borah. Now any questions you'd like to

speaker: SMC
words: ask me?

speaker: SMC
words: GRACE: I talked to her just before she came west in Washington, and she

speaker: SMC
words: said "Oh, Grace I can't see, I can't hear, and I can't walk." And she

speaker: SMC
words: was concerned that day for some books she was sending to the University

speaker: SMC
words: of Idaho library, and we got that squared away. And then, I think Lola

speaker: SMC
words: could tell us a little inside information on how she was supported those

speaker: SMC
words: last years. At the time of her husband's death there was an ample

speaker: SMC
words: inheritance, but you just live for so long and use your principal and

speaker: SMC
words: she just plain ran out of money. So, Lola why don't you tell about that.

speaker: SMC
words: LOLA: Oh yes. I'm proud of our state. When it got down to the bedrock,

speaker: SMC
words: it's the best kept secret in the state of Idaho. The state legislator

speaker: SMC
words: put up the money. The first year Harry Day and Harry Solen and Lynn

speaker: SMC
words: Driscoll, who was the banker from Boise, and a long-time friend, they

speaker: SMC
words: were old time friends, they put up the first money.

speaker: SMC
words: UNKNOWN 3: And Levin? Randall?

speaker: SMC
words: LOLA: Yes, that's right, and that they would continue. But the state of

speaker: SMC
words: Idaho said, "We're proud of the Borahs. They brought distinction and

speaker: SMC
words: honor to our state and we want to do it, but we'll keep it all a secret.

speaker: SMC
words: No one will know where it is coming from. So, it was a piece of

speaker: SMC
words: legislature that enacted it. Never appeared in the papers, I never heard

speaker: SMC
words: any of the legislators mention it at all, but I know that our own Harold

speaker: SMC
